summary: Jeff Atwood dissects Swoopo, a predatory auction site that combines elements of eBay, Woot, and slot machines to extract maximum money from users. The site charges 75 cents per bid while only raising prices by 15 cents, meaning users collectively pay far more than items are worth. Atwood explains how the site exploits the endowment effect—once users have invested in bidding, they irrationally continue rather than walk away. He argues Swoopo isn't really an auction site but a lottery or gambling operation, calling it 'pure, distilled evil in a business plan' that preys on human psychology while being almost certainly illegal in the US.
key_points: Swoopo's business model ensures the house always wins: a $229 iPod sold for $187.65 final price but generated $938.25 in bid fees, totaling $1,125.90
The 15-second auction extension on each bid prevents sniping but creates endless bidding wars that drain participants
The site exploits the endowment effect—a psychological phenomenon where people irrationally protect sunk costs rather than walking away
Swoopo offers increasingly predatory variants: penny auctions, cash auctions, and 'FreeBids' auctions that are essentially printing their own money
The company appears to drop-ship items after collecting payment, maintaining no inventory while reserving the right to substitute 'equivalent' items
money_quotes: So that $229 item ultimately sold for $1,125.90.
Even when you win, you can lose.
There's something else at work here, though, and it's almost an exploit of human nature itself.
So instead of doing the rational thing and walking away from a bad investment, you pour more money in, sending good money after bad.
It's pretty clear to me that Swoopo isn't an auction site.
The only way to win here is sheer dumb luck.
Or, of course, by not playing at all.
Swoopo is about as close to pure, distilled evil in a business plan as I've ever seen.
key_insight: Swoopo represents a masterclass in exploiting human psychology for profit, weaponizing the endowment effect to turn a fake auction into a gambling operation where the house always wins.
